column4: Finding the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island

column1:
In Brantingham1993, he references a "recent" Santa Barbara Yesterdays column in which it is suggested that the LW was an Alaskan Indian and people were upset. This definitely exists. It might refer to work by Marla Daily or a column addressing her work which would have been recent at the time.

Sara thinks this is an erroneous reference to Daily1989. Rachel thinks we just didn't find it because of the specificty of Brantingham1993's description.

Interesting clipping about IBD film but we don't know what paper it appeared in and we would certainly need reprint permission. Because of notes it was found with, we thought it was in the Sacramento Bee.  It definitely is not!

column3: ("an old bulletin reporting a former trip by the Smithsonian Institution to this island in the last century")
column13:
8/4/2015--RManuszak emailed Smithsonian
9/9/20915--RManuszak resent email inquiry to Smithsonian
9/10/2015-- ["Hello Rachel,
 I’m forwarding your email to the National Anthropological Archives as they may be able to assist you.  They receive a lot of email so it may take a few days before they get back in touch with you.  I hope this helps in your research."]
9/15/2015 RManuszak email w/ Adam Minakowski (Reference Archivist, National Anthropological Archives Smithsonian Institution Museum Support Center)--reply from Daisy Njoku [I browsed the Bryan’s bibliography  looking for possible origin of the bulletin he mentions in his article.  Three possibles stand out, though there may be more.
 1)      Burial Methods of the Southern California Islanders, by Hector Alliott of the South West Museum  –not sure where this is from, possibly from Bulletin of the Southern California Academy of Sciences, vol. 15, part 1; Jan. 1916.  Alliott  talks about burials,  heads "turned slightly towards the east"
 2)      Bulletin of the US Geological and Geographical Survey, vol. iii, no. 1 (no date given)
 And most annoying
 3)      -th Annual Report, Smithsonian institution – Expedition to San Nicolas  (no date given, and you’ll notice no number for the Annual Report either!)  If you have access to any of these volumes, you could browse the 1870s onward for any reference to this expedition.]
